title: 奉命谋杀
summary:
Arthur Calgary discusses the implications of Hester's words with Mr. Marshall, concluding that if a member of the family is guilty, the resulting suspicion will destroy their relationships and trust forever. Calgary expresses a motive to continue his investigation because he feels his previous actions failed to provide true justice for both the guilty and the innocent.

Marshall provides historical background on the family, explaining that Rachel Constable—the daughter of millionaire Rudolph Constable—and her husband Leo Constable established a wartime nursery at a house called Sun Angle (formerly Snake Angle) in 1940. Because Rachel could not have children, she adopted five children: Mary, Michael, Tina, Hester, and Jack Agglethorpe. While the children were well-educated and provided for, Jack was unruly, frequently stole money, and failed in multiple business ventures. Marshall reveals that Jack was secretly married to a young dancer from Dartmouth, a fact unknown to the rest of the family; she remarried an electrician shortly after Jack's death.

Regarding the night Mrs. Aigle was killed, Marshall states that Leo Aigle, Hester, Mary Dalton, and her disabled husband were present, along with Kirsten Lindström, a Swedish nurse. Michael and Tina were absent, and Mr. Aggert's secretary, Miss Vaughn, had left before the body was discovered. Calgary notes that Miss Vaughn and Mr. Aggert were reportedly planning an engagement. When Calgary asks about a motive, Marshall explains that no one stands to gain financially from the death because Mrs. Aigle set up discretionary trusts for the children, which are managed by three trustees: Marshall, Leo Aigle, and an American lawyer.
